breakClass: .ToList()/.ToArray() (or a nested correlated-collection projection) embedded inside a server-side EF Core 10 query â€” either in a `let` clause whose value is consumed by a server `select new {...}`/`where ... .Contains(...)`, or as an inline `Field = (from ... let X=db....ToList() ... select ...).ToList()` sub-collection â€” on an outer IQueryable that has NOT yet crossed an .AsEnumerable()/.ToList() materialization boundary. EF6 client-evaluated these; EF Core 10 throws "could not be translated" (500). Outer queries that are materialized first (var data2 = v.ToList(); from o in data2 ...) or sourced from db.X.AsEnumerable() are SAFE.
why: db.Customers source with no materialization boundary; let f = db.AssignedTos....ToList() is consumed by where f.Contains(empl.EmployeeId), forcing it server-side; the query is enumerated by .ToList() at line 508. EF Core 10 cannot translate the embedded .ToList() in the predicate. The SIBLING method at line 240 in the same file was explicitly fixed by rewriting let ...ToList()+Contains into db.X.Any(...) â€” this method was missed. Port regression.
reasoning:
I independently confirm this is a real EF Core 10 port-break (high severity). One correction to the claim's metadata: the enclosing method is GetAllMyLeads() at QuickNetCore/Controllers/LeadsController.cs:454, NOT followups. (followups() at line 266 simply calls GetAllMyLeads() at line 270: vmodel.totalleads = GetAllMyLeads().ToString();, so the broken query IS on a live request path.) The query body and line numbers cited are otherwise accurate.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. The outer IQueryable starts at line 488 `var v = (from a in db.Customers` (DbSet, no AsEnumerable). The `let f = db.AssignedTos.Where(cl => cl.CustomerID==a.CustomerID && ...).Select(cl => cl.EmployeeId).ToList()` (line 495) and `let g = ...FirstOrDefault()` (line 497) are CORRELATED subqueries (they reference a.CustomerID), so they are part of the composed server tree, not standalone top-level locals. The FIRST materialization boundary on the outer query is the `.ToList()` at line 508 (`.Where(o => o.ldate > crdate).ToList()`). The embedded `.ToList()` + `f.Contains(empl.EmployeeId)` at lines 495/500 sit entirely BEFORE that boundary, so they are translated as part of the SQL query. Not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. Genuinely server-side.

(b) THROWS 500 â€” confirmed. EF Core 10 cannot translate a List<T> materialized via .ToList() inside a let clause that is then consumed by a server-side WHERE predicate (`where ... f.Contains(empl.EmployeeId)`); it raises InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" at enumeration (the .ToList() on line 508) -> unhandled -> HTTP 500. This fires on every call regardless of data (it is a translation failure, not data-dependent), so it 500s on realistic data.

PORT REGRESSION â€” confirmed. The legacy EF6 source at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/LeadsController.cs:551-578 is byte-for-byte identical to the ported version (same `let f = ...ToList()`, same `f.Contains(empl.EmployeeId)`, same outer `.ToList()` at the matching offset). EF6's client-side LINQ evaluation tolerated the in-memory List Contains; EF Core 10 does not. The SIBLING method GetMyAMCDetails was already given exactly this fix (line 226-247, with an explicit comment at lines 239-242: "the original `let assign = ....ToList()` is not translatable in EF Core 10 ... rewritten as a server-translatable EXISTS"); GetAllMyLeads was missed.

Entity check (Models/Customer.cs:181): AssignedTo has long EmployeeId, long CustomerID, string Status, int ChkStatus, bool approve â€” so the Any(...) EXISTS rewrite translates cleanly and the predicate matches the original column-for-column. The output is identical because `f.Contains(empl.EmployeeId)` is true exactly when an active "Assigned", approve==false AssignedTo row exists for this customer with EmployeeId == empl.EmployeeId.

The proposed fix in the claim is correct and output-preserving.
